subsystem: Exiv2 image-metadata library: the Image::printIFDStructure method, which parses and pretty-prints the TIFF/EXIF Image File Directory (IFD) structure of an image file. It walks a TIFF directory chain read from a BasicIo stream, decoding each 12-byte directory entry (tag/type/count/offset) and recursively descending into sub-IFDs (ExifTag, SubIFDs, MakerNote, IPTC-NAA) to dump the structure to an output stream.
provenance: Identified by the Exiv2 namespace and API surface: Exiv2::PrintStructureOption, kpsBasic/kpsRecursive, DataBuf, BasicIo, MemIo, byteSwap2/byteSwap4, Internal::indent/stringFormat, IptcData::printStructure, Safe::add, makeSlice/makeSliceUntil, and the Error(kerTiffDirectoryTooLarge/kerInvalidTypeValue/kerInvalidMalloc/kerCorruptedMetadata) codes. The method signature Image::printIFDStructure and TIFF tag constants (0x8769 ExifTag, 0x014a SubIFDs, 0x83bb IPTCNAA, 0x927c MakerNote) are the exact Exiv2 image.cpp implementation. The sample banner names it primevul_train_2877_vuln / cwe835.
trust_boundary: The entire input is attacker-controlled: `io` is a BasicIo stream over an image file that a remote/untrusted party supplies. Every 16-bit and 32-bit value decoded from the stream (dirLength at line 16; tag/type/count/offset at lines 36-39; the next-directory `start` at line 176; recursion offsets at lines 121/135/146) is hostile and can be crafted. The only sanity gates are dirLength>500 (line 18), typeValid(type) (line 42), the allocate>io.size() check (line 69), and Safe::add for the IPTC branch (line 127). There is NO bound on recursion depth and NO cycle check on directory offsets.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The method takes an input stream `io`, an output stream `out`, a print `option` (basic vs recursive), a byte `start` offset into the file where this directory begins, a `bSwap` flag telling whether the file's byte order differs from the host (endianness), a fill character `c`, and a recursion `depth`. On entry it increments `depth` and sets `bFirst`, a flag used to print the column header only once. `start`, `bSwap`, and `depth` are all propagated on recursion, so `start` and `depth` are the key attacker-influenced controls: `start` selects which file bytes are parsed, and `depth` is meant to track how deep the recursion is (but, as seen below, is never used to stop it).

A fixed 32-byte scratch buffer `dir` is allocated to hold pieces of the directory as they are read from the stream (the 2-byte entry count, then 12-byte entries, then the 4-byte next-directory pointer). `bPrint` decides whether human-readable output is emitted for the basic and recursive options. The 32-byte size comfortably exceeds the largest single read (12 bytes), so `dir` itself is not the overflow surface; it is a staging area whose decoded contents drive everything downstream.

The outer do/while loop processes one IFD per iteration. It seeks to `start` and reads the 2-byte entry count into `dir`, decoding it (with endianness swap) into `dirLength` — the number of 12-byte directory entries that follow. It rejects directories claiming more than 500 entries by throwing kerTiffDirectoryTooLarge. This is the sole cap on entry count; up to 500 entries per directory are still processed, and this loop can re-enter many times via the next-directory pointer read at line 176.

On the first pass, if printing is enabled, a banner line is emitted with an indent proportional to `depth` and the file path. The `if(tooBig)` branch here is dead relative to the throw above (tooBig already threw at line 19), so it never prints. This block is purely cosmetic and carries no memory-safety contract.

The per-entry loop runs up to `dirLength` (≤500) times. Each iteration reads a 12-byte directory entry into `dir` and decodes the four TIFF fields: `tag` (2 bytes, what the entry is), `type` (2 bytes, the datum type), `count` (4 bytes, number of elements — fully attacker-controlled up to 2^32-1), and `offset` (4 bytes, either an inline value or a file offset to the data). `count` and `offset` are the primary hostile inputs that size buffers and select where subsequent reads land.

If the decoded `type` is not a recognized TIFF type, the code logs the bad value and throws kerInvalidTypeValue. This gate ensures `size` (computed below from the type) is well-defined; the in-code comment states it guards against a segfault. Setting start=0 before throwing is inert since the throw unwinds immediately.

what_the_code_does.code:
std::string sp  = "" ; // output spacer

                //prepare to print the value
                uint32_t kount  = isPrintXMP(tag,option) ? count // haul in all the data
                                : isPrintICC(tag,option) ? count // ditto
                                : isStringType(type)     ? (count > 32 ? 32 : count) // restrict long arrays
                                : count > 5              ? 5
                                : count
                                ;
                uint32_t pad    = isStringType(type) ? 1 : 0;
                uint32_t size   = isStringType(type) ? 1
                                : is2ByteType(type)  ? 2
                                : is4ByteType(type)  ? 4
                                : is8ByteType(type)  ? 8
                                : 1
                                ;
what_the_code_does.explanation: `kount` is how many elements to actually print: for XMP/ICC tags it is the full attacker `count` (unbounded); for string types it is capped at 32; otherwise capped at 5. `pad` reserves a trailing NUL byte for strings. `size` is the per-element byte width derived from the type (1/2/4/8). Note that for XMP/ICC the print count is NOT capped, which matters at lines 167-171 where `count` indexes/writes the buffer directly.

The required allocation size is computed in 64-bit arithmetic as size*count + pad + 20; the `(long long)` cast on `size` forces the multiplication into 64-bit so the *allocate* value cannot wrap (a fix for the referenced POC8 crash). It is then rejected if it exceeds the file size, so an attacker cannot force a giant allocation without a giant file. `buf` is allocated (minimum ~20 bytes), zeroed, and the 4 inline offset bytes (dir[8:11]) are copied in as the default value for short entries. The commented-out offset>io.size() check is a hint that offset itself is not otherwise validated here.

what_the_code_does.code:
const bool bOffsetIsPointer = count*size > 4;

                if ( bOffsetIsPointer ) {         // read into buffer
                    size_t   restore = io.tell();  // save
                    io.seek(offset,BasicIo::beg);  // position
                    io.read(buf.pData_,count*size);// read
                    io.seek(restore,BasicIo::beg); // restore
                }
what_the_code_does.explanation: When the data does not fit in the 4 inline bytes (count*size > 4), the value lives at file `offset`. The code saves the current stream position, seeks to `offset`, reads count*size bytes into `buf`, and restores. Critically `count*size` here is computed in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic (unlike the 64-bit `allocate`), so it can wrap; and `offset` is used verbatim without validating it lies within the file.

This block formats one output row: the file address of the entry, the tag/type names, count, and the value(s). Depending on the type it iterates `kount` elements reading from `buf` at k*size via byteSwap2/byteSwap4 (short/long/rational), or renders a string slice makeSlice(buf,0,kount). Because `buf` was sized to hold at least count*size bytes (line 72) and kount≤count, these buffer reads stay in bounds; `tagName(tag).c_str()` is consumed within the stringFormat call so the temporary string is still alive. The interesting parts are the recursion branches that follow.

For recursive mode, when the entry is an Exif sub-IFD, SubIFDs list, or an IFD-typed value, the code loops over `count` sub-offsets read from `buf` and recursively calls printIFDStructure at each `offset`. This is the central danger: `offset` is fully attacker-controlled and `depth` is passed through with no upper bound, so a crafted file can make the parser recurse arbitrarily deep (or into a cycle where directory A points to B points to A).

For the IPTC-NAA tag, it validates count+offset (using Safe::add, which guards against integer overflow) against the file size, then reads `count` bytes into a std::vector and hands the slice to IptcData::printStructure. The overflow-safe addition check is a hardening added for a known CVE, but note that if `count` is 0 the vector is empty and `&bytes[0]` dereferences a zero-length vector — undefined behavior — and `read_bytes` (a signed long from io.read) is fed to makeSliceUntil.

For MakerNote entries with count>10, it reads 10 bytes into a 20-byte stack buffer, NUL-terminates at index 10, and strcmp's the prefix against "Nikon". If it matches, it heap-allocates count-jump bytes (safe because count>10 guarantees no unsigned underflow of count-10), reads the embedded TIFF, wraps it in a MemIo, and recurses via printTiffStructure; delete[] correctly matches new[]. Otherwise it treats the value as an IFD and recurses via printIFDStructure at `offset` after seeking to file start. Both branches recurse with the same unbounded `depth`.

For XMP tags it writes a NUL terminator at buf.pData_[count] and prints buf as a C string; for ICC it writes `count` raw bytes. Because `buf` was sized allocate = size*count+pad+20 ≥ count+20 (size≥1), the index buf.pData_[count] and the count-byte write are within the allocation. The C-string print relies on the written NUL at index count being reached before any earlier unterminated garbage — the content between the read bytes and index count is zeroed by the memset, so this is in-bounds though it prints attacker-controlled bytes.

After the entry loop, if `start` is nonzero it reads the 4-byte next-directory pointer and decodes it into `start`; the do/while repeats while `start` is nonzero. Since `start` comes straight from attacker bytes with no requirement that it advance forward or differ from a previously visited directory, the chain can be crafted to loop indefinitely. Finally it prints the END marker, flushes, and decrements depth (the decrement is purely bookkeeping and does not limit recursion).

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Uncontrolled recursion / stack exhaustion. In recursive mode, printIFDStructure calls itself with an attacker-supplied `offset` and the same (merely incremented) `depth`, and there is no check like `if (depth > MAX) return`. An attacker crafts a sub-IFD whose offset points at another sub-IFD, forming an arbitrarily long or cyclic chain (A→B→A). Each recursion consumes a stack frame plus a 32-byte DataBuf and other locals; a small file can drive recursion until the stack is exhausted, crashing the process (DoS). The same unbounded recursion exists at line 159 (printIFDStructure) and line 154 (printTiffStructure). The implementation MUST enforce a maximum recursion depth and/or track visited offsets to break cycles.

Infinite loop via attacker-controlled next-directory pointer. `start` is re-read from the file each iteration with no requirement that it move forward or that a given directory offset be visited only once. A file whose directory-A next-pointer references directory B whose next-pointer references directory A (or any offset that re-reads a directory with a nonzero self-referential next-pointer) makes the do/while loop at line 178 spin forever, hanging the process. Because it also emits output each pass, it can additionally flood the output stream. The implementation MUST detect already-visited directory offsets (or require strictly increasing offsets) to guarantee termination.

Zero-length container indexing (undefined behavior). For an IPTC-NAA entry an attacker can set count=0. The Safe::add(count,offset)>io.size() gate passes (0+offset likely ≤ size), then std::vector<byte> bytes(0) is empty and `&bytes[0]` evaluates operator[] on an empty vector, which is undefined behavior — in practice a pointer into a zero-sized allocation that is then passed to io.read and makeSliceUntil. Even for count>0, `read_bytes` is a signed long from io.read whose value flows into makeSliceUntil as the slice length; a short read on a truncated file leaves the invariant read_bytes<=count only if io.read never returns a negative/oversized value. The implementation MUST early-return when count==0 and use bytes.data(), and must treat read_bytes as an unsigned, bounds-checked length.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
const bool bOffsetIsPointer = count*size > 4;

                if ( bOffsetIsPointer ) {         // read into buffer
                    size_t   restore = io.tell();  // save
                    io.seek(offset,BasicIo::beg);  // position
                    io.read(buf.pData_,count*size);// read
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: 32-bit multiplication overflow inconsistent with the 64-bit allocation. `buf` is sized from the 64-bit `allocate = (long long)size*count + pad + 20` (line 68), but `bOffsetIsPointer` and the read length here use `count*size` computed in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic. With a large `count` and size≥2, count*size can wrap modulo 2^32 to a small value: the read then fills only that small wrapped number of bytes while later loops (lines 94-112) index buf using kount and k*size derived from the true type width. Because `allocate` uses the un-wrapped 64-bit product, the io.size() gate would normally reject such large products, so this is a latent inconsistency rather than an immediate overflow, but the two size computations diverging is fragile: the implementation MUST compute count*size once, in 64-bit, and reuse it for the buffer size, the pointer test, and the read length.

Unbounded XMP element count and C-string printing of attacker bytes. Unlike other types, `kount` for XMP is the full `count` (line 51) with no 32/5 cap, and here buf.pData_[count] is written and the buffer is streamed as a NUL-terminated C string. The write index `count` is in bounds only because allocate ≥ count+20; if a future change let `size` be 0 for some XMP type, allocate could equal count+pad+20 still ≥ count, but any weakening of the allocate formula turns buf.pData_[count]=0 into an out-of-bounds write (CWE-787). Additionally the printed C string exposes raw file bytes up to the first NUL. The implementation MUST keep allocate strictly greater than the largest index it writes and should bound XMP output length rather than trusting `count`.

summary: This is Exiv2's Image::printIFDStructure TIFF/EXIF directory dumper operating entirely on attacker-controlled image bytes. The dominant, matching-the-cwe835-label flaw is uncontrolled recursion: printIFDStructure/printTiffStructure recurse on attacker offsets with no depth cap and no visited-offset tracking, and the do/while(start) loop follows an attacker next-directory pointer with no cycle guard — either yields stack-exhaustion or hang DoS (CVE-2018-19108 family). Secondary latent issues include zero-length std::vector indexing in the IPTC-NAA branch and a 32-bit count*size computation that diverges from the 64-bit allocation size.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: printIFDStructure(BasicIo&, std::ostream&, PrintStructureOption, uint32_t start, bool bSwap, char c, int depth) — signature (line 2)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Recursive entry point; `start`/`depth` attacker-influenced and depth is never bounded (uncontrolled recursion).
depth is incremented/decremented for indentation only, never compared to a maximum, so it does not limit recursion.
Fixed 32-byte staging buffer; all reads into it are ≤12 bytes, so no overflow of dir itself.
2-byte read into 32-byte buffer; dirLength is a count value, bounded next by the >500 check.
Entry-count cap; valid guard limiting the inner loop to ≤500 iterations.
Bounded by dirLength≤500; signed int index cannot overflow at that range.
12-byte read into 32-byte buffer; count/offset are the hostile values but are handled at their use sites.
Validation gate ensuring size is well-defined; correct hardening, no contract violated here.
XMP/ICC path uses full attacker count with no cap; feeds the buf.pData_[count] write/print at lines 167-171.
Per-element width from a validated type; bounded small constant.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: long long allocate = (long long)size*count + pad+20; if (allocate>io.size()) throw (lines 68-71)
reviewed_anchors.reason: 64-bit product plus file-size gate correctly prevents the giant-allocation crash (POC8).
Copies 4 inline bytes into a buffer of ≥20 bytes; in bounds.
count*size is 32-bit and can wrap, diverging from the 64-bit allocate size; offset used without a file-bounds check.
kount≤count and buf holds ≥count*size bytes, so k*size accesses stay within the allocation.
Recurses on attacker offset with unbounded depth and no cycle detection — stack exhaustion.
count==0 makes &bytes[0] undefined behavior; read_bytes signed length passed to makeSliceUntil.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: MakerNote branch: byte bytes[20]; io.read(bytes,10); bytes[10]=0; new byte[count-jump] (lines 140-155)
reviewed_anchors.reason: count>10 guard prevents count-10 underflow; 10-byte read + NUL at index 10 fits the 20-byte buffer; new[]/delete[] paired.
Additional unbounded recursion sites feeding the same stack-exhaustion class (covered by the recursion finding).
Writes at index count and prints attacker bytes as a C string; in bounds only while allocate>count holds.
count-byte write from a buffer of allocate≥count bytes; in bounds, output-only.
Attacker next-directory pointer with no monotonicity/visited check can loop forever.

findings.title: Uncontrolled recursion into sub-IFDs causes stack exhaustion (DoS)
findings.invariant: Recursion into sub-IFDs must be bounded: enforce a maximum `depth` (and/or record visited directory offsets) and refuse to descend when the limit is hit or an offset has already been visited.
findings.failure_mode: In kpsRecursive mode a crafted image with an ExifTag/SubIFDs/IFD-typed entry whose offset points to another sub-IFD (or forms a cycle A→B→A) makes printIFDStructure call itself with attacker-controlled `offset` and an unbounded `depth`. Each frame consumes stack plus a 32-byte DataBuf; a small file drives recursion until the stack is exhausted and the process crashes. The same class exists at the MakerNote recursion (lines 154, 159).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no `if (depth > MAX_DEPTH) return;` guard and no visited-offset set anywhere in the call chain; verify a self-referential sub-IFD file overflows the stack.

findings.title: Attacker-controlled next-directory pointer allows infinite loop
findings.bug_class: Loop with unreachable exit condition (infinite loop)
findings.invariant: The next-directory offset chain must terminate: require strictly increasing offsets or track visited directory offsets and stop when one repeats.
findings.failure_mode: `start` is re-read from the file every iteration with no requirement that it advance or be unique. A file whose directory next-pointer references a directory whose next-pointer references it back (or any nonzero self/cyclic offset) makes the do/while at line 178 spin forever, hanging the process and flooding the output stream — a denial of service from a single small crafted file.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no visited-offset tracking or monotonicity requirement guards the do/while; verify a two-directory cyclic next-pointer file hangs.

findings.title: Zero-length std::vector indexed via &bytes[0] in IPTC-NAA branch
findings.anchor: std::vector<byte> bytes(count); io.read(&bytes[0], count); makeSliceUntil(&bytes[0], read_bytes)
findings.bug_class: Undefined behavior: operator[] on empty container / unchecked signed read length
findings.invariant: Never take &vec[0] on a possibly-empty vector; early-return when count==0 and use vec.data(). Treat io.read's signed return as a bounds-checked unsigned length before slicing.
findings.failure_mode: For an IPTCNAA tag with count==0 the Safe::add(count,offset)>io.size() gate passes, the vector is empty, and `&bytes[0]` invokes operator[](0) on an empty vector — undefined behavior — before being passed to io.read and makeSliceUntil. With count>0 a truncated file yields a short `read_bytes` (signed long) whose value is fed as the slice length to IptcData::printStructure without re-validation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm behavior when count==0 (whether &bytes[0] is reached) and whether read_bytes can be negative or is bounds-checked before makeSliceUntil.

findings.title: 32-bit count*size read length diverges from 64-bit allocation size
findings.code:
const bool bOffsetIsPointer = count*size > 4;

if ( bOffsetIsPointer ) {         // read into buffer
    size_t   restore = io.tell();  // save
    io.seek(offset,BasicIo::beg);  // position
    io.read(buf.pData_,count*size);// read
    io.seek(restore,BasicIo::beg); // restore
}
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow (32-bit multiply) inconsistent with buffer sizing
findings.invariant: The value size*count must be computed once in 64-bit and reused for the allocation, the bOffsetIsPointer test, and the read length so the read never disagrees with the buffer size.
findings.failure_mode: `buf` is sized from the 64-bit `allocate` (line 68), but `count*size` here is 32-bit unsigned and can wrap modulo 2^32; the pointer test and read length then use a different (wrapped) value than the buffer was sized for. The io.size() gate on `allocate` normally rejects the large products that would wrap, so this is a latent inconsistency rather than an immediate overflow, but the duplicated, differently-typed size computations are fragile and could become exploitable if the allocate guard changes.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether any reachable count/size makes 32-bit count*size wrap while 64-bit allocate stays ≤ io.size(); unify the computation in 64-bit.

findings.title: Uncapped XMP element count drives buf index-write and C-string dump
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write / over-read on buffer indexed by attacker count
findings.invariant: The buffer must always be strictly larger than the largest index written (buf.size_ > count), and XMP output length must be bounded rather than trusting `count`.
findings.failure_mode: For XMP tags `kount` is the full attacker `count` (line 51), and here buf.pData_[count] is written and buf is streamed as a NUL-terminated C string. Today allocate = size*count+pad+20 ≥ count+20 keeps the index in bounds and the zeroed tail bounds the print, but the safety depends entirely on that allocate formula; any weakening (e.g. a type yielding size 0, or a changed +20 slack) turns buf.pData_[count]=0 into a one-byte out-of-bounds write and the C-string print into an over-read of attacker data.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm allocate is always > count for every XMP-eligible type/size combination, and that the printed region is always NUL-terminated within the allocation.

audit_checklist: Add a hard recursion-depth cap (and/or a visited-offset set) in printIFDStructure/printTiffStructure before every recursive call at lines 122, 154, and 159.
Detect cycles in the next-directory pointer chain (lines 174-178): reject non-advancing or already-visited `start` offsets so the do/while must terminate.
Early-return when count==0 in the IPTC-NAA branch and use bytes.data() instead of &bytes[0]; validate read_bytes as an unsigned, ≤count length before makeSliceUntil (lines 133-138).
Compute size*count once in 64-bit and reuse it for allocate, bOffsetIsPointer, and the io.read length so the read can never disagree with the buffer size (lines 68-80).
Validate `offset` against io.size() before every seek/read (lines 79, 132, 146, 159), not only for the IPTC branch.
Ensure buf allocation is always strictly greater than `count` before buf.pData_[count]=0 for XMP, and bound XMP/ICC output length (lines 167-171).
